posts.text:
De la Espriella's victory in Colombia could accelerate energy projects, but major hurdles remain

▪️De la Espriella win may restart oil exploration
▪️Constitutional limits block fracking, licensing
▪️Gas supply critical as reserve margins tighten

🖥️Full story: https://t.co/Qv7xL0LnB6
posts.summary: De la Espriella's electoral victory in Colombia could restart oil exploration activities, though constitutional restrictions on fracking and licensing will continue to constrain energy project expansion. Gas supply challenges amid tightening reserve margins add urgency to resolving these regulatory obstacles.

posts.text: The Strait of Hormuz is no longer simply “open” or “closed.” Analysts warn that GPS spoofing, insurance hurdles, sanctions compliance, and new Iranian transit rules are creating major risks for physical oil flows—risks largely ignored by futures markets. #Oil #Hormuz
posts.summary: Multiple operational and regulatory obstacles—including GPS spoofing, insurance complications, sanctions compliance, and new Iranian transit rules—are degrading the functionality of the Strait of Hormuz for oil shipments, yet financial markets appear to be underpricing these emerging risks to physical crude flows.

COT on #commodities covering managed money positioning in week to 16 June.
Another week of broad selling led by Brent, soybeans, corn, sugar and cotton, while some demand emerged in precious metals, in gold driven by short covering during the slump to near USD 4,000 while the https://t.co/GUyfDvNoby
posts.summary: Managed money positioned for further selling in Brent crude during the week ending June 16, contributing to broader commodity weakness across energy and agricultural markets. The pullback reflected sustained liquidation pressure in oil alongside declines in soybeans, corn, sugar, and cotton.

posts.text:
Global energy demand growth was met by diverse range of sources in 2025 – led by solar & then gas.

While growth slowed from the previous year against a complex economic and geopolitical backdrop, electricity use outpaced overall demand.

More 👉 https://t.co/c9Z4hPfenX https://t.co/ypbEJSXgJO
posts.summary: Global energy demand growth in 2025 was primarily met by solar and gas rather than oil, suggesting continued displacement of oil's market share. The slowdown in overall demand growth against economic and geopolitical headwinds could further pressure oil demand in the near term.

posts.text: Hydrogen’s credibility may be recovering. Germany has committed €1.3 billion to three Danish green hydrogen projects, helping unlock production, demand, and infrastructure simultaneously. The deal could become Europe’s first functioning cross-border hydrogen market. #Hydrogen
posts.summary: Germany's €1.3 billion investment in Danish green hydrogen projects signals growing European commitment to hydrogen as an alternative energy source, potentially reducing long-term dependence on traditional oil and gas markets. The development of Europe's first cross-border hydrogen infrastructure could accelerate the energy transition and create competitive pressure on conventional energy sectors.

posts.text: Non-oil export revenues totaled $2.27 billion in the first quarter, 15.5% more than in the same period of 2025, according to the central bank’s data. Total crude export revenues last year were $17.1 billion, according to the bank.
posts.summary: Venezuela's non-oil exports grew 15.5% year-over-year in Q1, reaching $2.27 billion, while crude export revenues totaled $17.1 billion for the previous year, indicating that oil remains the dominant source of export revenue despite diversification efforts.

posts.text: Venezuela has seen a rebound this year in its crude production and exports to the United States and Europe after Washington eased sanctions and foreign companies expanded oil and gas projects after the U.S. ouster of President Nicolas Maduro in January.
posts.summary: Venezuela's crude production and exports have rebounded in 2025 following U.S. sanctions relief and expanded foreign oil and gas projects after Maduro's ouster, signaling increased supply to key markets. This production recovery could help ease global oil supply concerns and potentially put downward pressure on crude prices.

posts.text: Climate resilience is becoming a core investment priority as weather-related losses mount worldwide. London is positioning itself as the leading hub for green finance, clean energy investment, and carbon markets, arguing climate action is now an economic necessity. #NetZero
posts.summary: Climate resilience is becoming a major investment focus globally, with London emerging as a leading financial hub for green energy and carbon markets, potentially redirecting capital away from traditional fossil fuel investments toward climate-related solutions. This shift reflects a broader market trend positioning climate action as economically necessary rather than optional.

posts.text:
In Western Canada, the gas-directed rig count fell from 58 to 56 rigs, while the oil-directed rig count gained eight to 127 rigs, for the week ended June 18, according to Baker Hughes data.
https://t.co/XuJ9JPeESa
posts.summary: Oil-directed rig activity in Western Canada increased to 127 rigs for the week ended June 18, gaining eight rigs, while gas-directed rigs declined slightly from 58 to 56, indicating a continued shift toward oil exploration and production in the region.

posts.text: More than €23 billion ($26.3B) has flowed into Greece’s energy sector in just one month as investors position for Europe’s shift away from Russian gas. Massive investments in power grids, renewables, and infrastructure are strengthening Greece’s role as a regional energy hub.
posts.summary: Greece is attracting substantial capital investment in energy infrastructure and renewables as Europe reduces dependence on Russian gas, positioning the country as a regional energy hub. While the investments focus primarily on power grids and renewables rather than oil specifically, they reflect broader market shifts away from fossil fuel reliance.

posts.text: A U.S.-Iran MOU may have eased fears temporarily, but Oilprice warns the real threat is dwindling global oil inventories. As “tank bottoms” approach and Hormuz traffic remains restricted, a supply crunch could trigger a new surge in crude prices. #Oil #Iran #Hormuz #EnergyMarkets
posts.summary: Global oil inventories are declining to critical levels, and with Strait of Hormuz traffic restrictions in place, a supply shortage could drive crude prices significantly higher despite temporary relief from U.S.-Iran negotiations. The combination of depleting reserves and reduced shipping capacity presents a material risk to oil market stability.

posts.text: Tough week to project US gasoline demand. Solstice week 2025 saw 9.688-mil b/d, which was easily the highest weekly number of the year. In 2022, $5/gallon gas led to a miserable figure of 8.5-mil b/d. Difference of 1.1-mil b/d is more than most countries consume.
posts.summary: US gasoline demand hit a record weekly high of 9.688 million barrels per day during the winter solstice week of 2025, significantly outpacing the depressed demand levels seen during the 2022 price spike, indicating strong current fuel consumption despite recent economic uncertainties.

posts.text:
I see little chance (probably zero) of a US refiner buying Iranian oil over next 60 days. So why did the White House made such an emphatic move to waive sanctions on US purchases of Iranian oil?

a) Gesture to Tehran with little consequence?

posts.summary: The White House's decision to waive sanctions on US purchases of Iranian oil appears unlikely to have immediate market impact, as US refiners are unlikely to actually buy Iranian crude in the near term, raising questions about whether the move was primarily a diplomatic gesture toward Tehran.

posts.text: US put ~9.1 million barrels of SPR oil into the market last week, the 2nd largest weekly release ever (equal to ~1.3 million b/d). Over the last 8 weeks, the US has been releasing >1m b/d on average.
posts.summary: The US released approximately 9.1 million barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve last week at a rate of 1.3 million barrels per day, marking the second-largest weekly release on record and part of a sustained effort averaging over 1 million barrels per day over the past two months. This substantial supply injection aims to increase oil availability in the market.

posts.text: Perhaps more important than the 9.1-million barrel drop in the SPR last week, the DOE awarded only 500,000 barrels in a sales process for 40-million barrels of crude. Sole accepted bid was Vitol. Market structure no longer supports replacing oil with swollen volumes.
posts.summary: The U.S. Department of Energy's Strategic Petroleum Reserve auction received minimal interest, with only 500,000 barrels accepted out of 40 million offered to a single bidder, suggesting the oil market currently lacks sufficient demand to absorb large replacement volumes. This weak auction response indicates structural challenges in the market's ability to replenish the SPR at historical scales.

posts.text:

posts.summary: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has caused the largest oil supply disruption in history, with Gulf producers losing over 1.3 billion barrels, though global oil supplies have adjusted to help compensate for the shortage.

posts.text:
What are the key forces shaping the future of global energy and oil markets?

In this latest article, HE Haitham Al Ghais, #OPEC Secretary General, outlines six pivotal trends from the World Oil Outlook 2026, including energy security, rising global demand, investment needs,
posts.summary: OPEC's Secretary General identifies six pivotal trends shaping global oil markets, emphasizing energy security, rising global demand, and investment needs as key forces influencing the sector's future. These factors collectively highlight critical considerations for the outlook of global energy and oil markets.

posts.text: BREAKING: U.S. SPR drops 9 million barrels to 331.2 million, 208.2mb of sour, 123.0mb of sweet crude, lowest level since July, 1983.
posts.summary: The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve has fallen to its lowest level in over 40 years at 331.2 million barrels following a 9 million barrel decline, potentially limiting the government's ability to respond to future oil supply disruptions. The reserve now holds 208.2 million barrels of sour crude and 123.0 million barrels of sweet crude.

posts.text: Twelve’s AirPlant One, which it bills as the first commercial-scale facility in the U.S. to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), has begun operations in Moses Lake, WA, the company said June 10. Referred to by Twelve as E-Jet fuel, it is an alternative to traditional jet fuel
posts.summary: Twelve's new commercial-scale sustainable aviation fuel facility in Moses Lake, Washington has begun producing E-Jet fuel as an alternative to traditional jet fuel. The facility marks the first commercial-scale SAF production operation in the U.S., potentially offering competition to conventional aviation fuel markets.

This supply glut could pressure global oil prices downward in the near term.

posts.text: So US companies can now buy Iranian crude. There were some piecemeal sales of a few barrels from 2021-2023 but the last significant years for Iranian imports were 1979 (297k/day); 1978 (554k) and 1977 (530k). Guessing the importers were wearing bell-bottoms.

The resumption of Iranian oil imports could have implications for US oil supply dynamics, though the text does not specify potential market impacts.

posts.text: US avg. retail diesel now $5.013/gal with sharply lower numbers soon. But average street prices can be 50cts/gal to $1/gal higher than what large fleets pay, since most buy their fuel on a cost-plus basis. Retail diesel averages are among the most misleading bench marks.
posts.summary: US retail diesel averages around $5.01 per gallon but may decline soon, though these figures significantly overstate actual costs paid by large fleets due to cost-plus purchasing arrangements that bypass higher street prices. The gap between retail and fleet pricing—potentially 50 cents to $1 per gallon—makes retail diesel benchmarks unreliable indicators of true market conditions.

posts.text:
It may only last 60 days (I doubt it; likely would become permanent), but the US Treasury’s waiver is effectively rolling back 40-year plus of American oil sanctions against Iran. 

Even US refiners are allowed to import Iranian oil and pay for it in greenback!!! https://t.co/nG7xDt1Fwj
posts.summary: The US Treasury has issued a waiver that temporarily lifts decades-long sanctions on Iranian oil, allowing US refiners to directly import Iranian crude and pay in dollars, which could significantly reshape global oil markets if the measure becomes permanent. This represents a major shift in American energy policy toward Iran after more than 40 years of restrictions.

posts.text:
Heatwaves can put major strain on energy systems as demand for cooling sends power use soaring.
 
In this 2025 podcast, IEA’s Brian Motherway looks at the rapid growth in air conditioning demand globally – and how to keep cool without overloading grids 👉 https://t.co/mDJZ2DMM9d https://t.co/Qcj5sHh4dL
posts.summary: Heatwaves drive increased air conditioning demand globally, which strains power grids and raises electricity consumption. While the post does not directly address oil market implications, higher cooling demand could increase energy system strain and potentially affect overall energy commodity markets.

posts.text:
Crucially, the general license not only allows importers of Iranian oil to pay Tehran (including via previously sanctioned entities), **but also do so in U.S. dollars!**

For 60 days at least, Washington is allowing Tehran back in the dollar club to repatriate its petrodollars.
posts.summary: The U.S. general license permits Iranian oil importers to pay Tehran in U.S. dollars for at least 60 days, effectively allowing Iran temporary access to dollar-denominated revenue from oil sales. This move restores Iran's ability to conduct petrodollar transactions through the U.S. financial system, a significant shift from previous sanctions restrictions.

posts.text:

🔷 UK crude output has slumped to 583,000 b/d

posts.summary: A potential change in UK leadership could create opportunities for approval of pending oil and gas projects (Rosebank, Cambo, and Jackdaw) that might help reverse the country's declining crude output, which has fallen to 583,000 barrels per day.

posts.text:

posts.summary: South Korea is increasing crude oil imports from Canada as part of a broader diversification strategy, with H1 imports expected to reach 8.16 million barrels. The move reflects efforts to reduce dependence on traditional suppliers and secure stable long-term energy sources.

posts.text: We’re expanding into the power-for-Al business to deliver on-site electricity to a next-generation @Microsoft data center, expected to be one of the largest U.S. power projects of its kind. posts.text:
Another important caveat to these exit flows.

While loadings have indeed begun to rise, led above all by the UAE (master of the shadow transits), those loadings are still running at less than half the pace of these boosted exits.

Inherently unsustainable without more ships in. https://t.co/UvYM6gr9Js
posts.summary: Exit flows from oil storage are outpacing the rate at which new shipments are being loaded, particularly via UAE transits, creating an unsustainable situation that will require additional incoming supplies to balance.
